title: How to Work with Childhood Memories That Surface Under Psilocybin
body:
How to Work with Childhood Memories That Surface Under Psilocybin

So, you’ve taken psilocybin, and suddenly a flood of childhood memories comes rushing up. Maybe it’s a specific moment: a birthday party that felt lonely, a scolding from a parent that still stings, or even a warm afternoon with a friend long gone. These memories can feel vivid, intense, sometimes confusing. You’re not alone in this. Many people notice how psilocybin can crack open old wounds or forgotten corners of the past. The question is: how do you work with these memories when they show up?

Here’s the thing, working with childhood memories under psilocybin isn’t about pushing them away or pretending they don’t hurt. It’s about meeting them with kindness and curiosity, even when they’re tough. I’m going to walk you through some practical steps to make this process safer and more useful, based on years of experience guiding people through conscious healing.

Why Childhood Memories Surface

Psilocybin affects your brain’s default mode network, loosening rigid patterns of thought. This can make old, buried memories more accessible, especially those connected to emotional pain or unresolved experiences from childhood. Think of it as your mind unlocking a dusty attic room where you stored old stuff. Some of it might be forgotten toys, some might be broken things you’d rather not see, but it’s all there.

When these memories pop up, your emotional brain is often in the driver’s seat. That’s why you might feel overwhelmed or flooded. It’s not just remembering - it’s re-experiencing. Know what I mean?

Step 1: Set Your Space and Intention

Before you even take psilocybin, setting a clear intention around what you want from the journey helps. You don’t have to say, “I want to heal my childhood,” but being open to what comes up is key. Having a safe, quiet space is essential. A spot where you can sit or lie down comfortably, with minimal distractions. I usually recommend having a meditation zafu cushion (paid link) nearby. It helps keep your body comfortable and your mind steady.

Also, have water, some light snacks, and maybe a trusted friend or sitter in another room, just in case. Knowing someone is there for you can make a huge difference.

Step 2: Ground Yourself When Memories Rush In

When a childhood memory surfaces, your first impulse might be to get lost in it, or maybe you want to run away from it. Both are natural. Instead, try to ground yourself by focusing on your breath, your body, or your immediate surroundings. Slow, deep breaths. Feel the cushion beneath you or the floor under your feet. Sometimes, lying on an acupressure mat and pillow set (paid link) can help you stay anchored in the present. It’s like telling your body: “I’m safe right now.”

Here’s a practical tip: When the memory feels intense, say quietly to yourself, “This is a memory. I am not in that moment right now.” It sounds simple, but it can create a little space between you and the emotion.

Step 3: Witness Without Judgment

Childhood memories often come with a heavy load of judgment: “I was so stupid,” or “I never deserved love.” Psilocybin can help you see these thoughts as just thoughts, not facts. When you notice judgment creeping in, try to step back and observe it. Imagine you’re watching a scene in a movie rather than living it.

This is easier said than done. Truth is, old wounds hurt, and the mind wants to protect you by shutting down or distracting you. But if you can stay present, even for a few breaths, you’ll find the memory loses some of its grip.

Step 4: Allow Emotions to Flow

Crying, trembling, laughing, or even silence - let it all happen. Emotions tied to childhood memories might have been stuffed down for years. Psilocybin opens the floodgates. You don’t have to fix anything right now. Just feel. Honestly, it’s okay to just be with the feelings without trying to make sense of them.

Remember: emotions are energy moving through your system. If you hold them back, they get stuck. If you let them move, they start to loosen.

Step 5: Make a Gentle Record

After the experience, journaling can be incredibly helpful. Writing down what you saw, felt, or thought without censoring yourself helps bring clarity. I recommend using a guided meditation journal (paid link) that prompts you to reflect on your inner experience in a structured way.

Don’t worry about grammar or neatness. This is just for you. Some people find drawing or voice recording more helpful. Whatever feels natural.

Step 6: Seek Support If Needed

Childhood wounds sometimes tap into complex trauma or deeply ingrained patterns. If memories are overwhelming or you feel stuck, it’s okay to reach out for professional help. A therapist trained in trauma-informed care can help you integrate these experiences safely. You’re not supposed to do this alone.

Also, I’ve written about how to integrate your psychedelic experiences, which might give you more tools for the days and weeks after your journey.

Stories From the Field

Take Sarah, for example. She came to me after a psilocybin session where she’d suddenly remembered a moment when her father ignored her birthday. The memory was vivid: the empty room, the silence, her young self waiting by the door. Instead of running from it, we worked on grounding techniques and gentle witness. Sarah allowed herself to cry, then journaled about how that moment shaped her sense of worth. Months later, she told me she felt “less haunted” by those feelings and had started talking openly with her family.

Or Mark, who remembered a childhood fight with his brother that ended with harsh words he’d never forgiven. The psilocybin experience helped him see the fight from a new angle, not as a personal failure but as a moment of two kids struggling. It opened a door for him to reach out and heal the relationship.

These stories aren’t rare. They’re proof that working with childhood memories under psilocybin can lead to real, concrete healing if you’re willing to face them with care.

Tips for Preparing Yourself

  • Practice meditation or mindfulness regularly. This builds your emotional resilience so memories don’t feel so overwhelming.
  • Keep a journal handy in the days leading up to your journey. Write about any childhood themes or feelings you notice coming up.
  • Try some gentle bodywork or somatic practices. Even simple stretches can help you stay connected to your body.

Here’s a bonus: I’ve found that spending 10-15 minutes on an acupressure mat before your session can help calm your nervous system. It’s like giving your body a mini-reset. Seriously, check out an acupressure mat and pillow set (paid link). It’s a simple tool but can make a big difference.

After the Journey: Integration Practices

Once the psilocybin wears off, the work isn’t done. Memories might still feel raw or confusing. This is where integration comes in. You can:

  • Keep journaling to track your feelings over time.
  • Talk with trusted friends or a therapist about what came up.
  • Practice gentle self-care: baths, walks, or even something like a meditation zafu cushion (paid link) to support your meditation practice.

Also, reading stories or books about similar journeys can help you feel less alone. A book like A Really Good Day by Ayelet Waldman (paid link) offers a personal look at mental health and psychedelic healing that many find inspiring.

For scientific context, check out this study on psilocybin and emotional processing. It helps explain why these memories can feel so vivid and how the brain is rewired during the experience.

Final Thoughts

Look, childhood memories can be tricky. They can bring pain, but they also hold keys to understanding yourself better. Psilocybin opens a door, but you still have to walk through it. That takes courage. You don’t have to be perfect. You don’t have to “fix” anything overnight. Just showing up, breathing, and allowing what comes is already a big step.

If you want more resources, reflections, and tools, you can always visit my site. I keep adding guides and support for conscious healing.

Does that land? Working with childhood memories under psilocybin is a process, not an event. Be patient with yourself. You’re doing important work.

Disclaimer: This content is for educational purposes only and does not constitute medical advice. Always consult a qualified healthcare provider before making changes to your health regimen.
